current_fact.fact_text: On March 26, 1885, the Métis people of the District of Saskatchewan, led by Louis Riel, began the North-West Rebellion against the Canadian government. It was a brief but intense uprising defending land, language, and identity on the prairies, and it still shapes debates about Indigenous rights in Canada today.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

current_fact.match_reason: An Indigenous-led rebellion for land and autonomy resonates with an episode about Tuareg music, exile, and resistance in another part of the world.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 1971, leaders in East Pakistan proclaimed independence from Pakistan, beginning the Bangladesh Liberation War. What started as a declaration in a tense, rain-soaked Dhaka would soon reshape South Asia’s map and give birth to a new nation rooted in language and culture.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A profound national rebirth is paired with an episode about immersion, transformation, and moving through vast, unpredictable waters.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 1970, South Vietnamese president Nguyễn Văn Thiệu launched a land reform program aimed at breaking up large estates and giving more farmers their own plots. In a country ravaged by war, it was an attempt to quietly redraw the social map by changing who owned the soil under their feet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A policy trying to reshape life on the land connects with a contemplative episode about slowing down and re-rooting on a ‘slow planet.’

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 1979, Anwar Sadat, Menachem Begin, and Jimmy Carter signed the Egypt–Israel peace treaty in Washington, D.C. After generations of war and suspicion, a document just a few pages long officially ended a state of conflict between two neighbors who share both deserts and deep histories.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: A landmark peace agreement about changing perspectives and borders fits an episode themed around travel, perspective shifts, and gentle reconciliation with change.

queue.fact_text: Also on March 26, 1979, an Interflug Ilyushin Il-18 crashed during a rejected takeoff at Luanda’s Quatro de Fevereiro Airport in Angola, killing ten people. The accident revealed how a few seconds of confusion on a runway can unravel into catastrophe, even for experienced aircrews.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A tragedy in the sky is matched with an episode about respect for nature’s risks and the fragility of moving through extreme environments.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 1955, Pan Am Flight 845/26 lost an engine and was forced to ditch in the cold Pacific Ocean off the coast of Oregon. The crew managed a controlled water landing, and most passengers survived hours in life rafts before rescue planes spotted them on the swells.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: An aircraft gently ‘landing’ on the sea and passengers floating in rafts connects with an episode that meditates on immersion, water, and suspension.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 1991, Argentina, Brazil, Uruguay, and Paraguay signed the Treaty of Asunción, creating Mercosur, a South American common market. The pact turned old rivalries into trade routes, knitting together economies with everything from soybeans to samba crossing new borders.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: A regional economic union about shared markets and cultural exchange pairs with an episode on Afrobeat’s cross-border musical fusion and anti-colonial spirit.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 1991, Singapore Airlines Flight 117 was hijacked by four armed men and forced to land at Changi Airport. In a tense overnight standoff, Singaporean commandos stormed the plane in minutes, killing the hijackers and rescuing all hostages in a rare, textbook counter-terrorism operation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A high-stakes hijacking and counter-terror operation matches the intensity and political edge of a Rage Against the Machine-focused episode.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 2005, between 200,000 and 300,000 people marched through Taipei to protest China’s Anti-Secession Law. Streets filled with a sea of flags and homemade signs as citizens used sheer numbers and noise to push back against the threat of forceful unification.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

queue.match_reason: A mass protest against being politically ‘boxed in’ fits with an episode about unboxing identities and resisting imposed labels.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 2010, the South Korean corvette ROKS Cheonan broke apart and sank near the maritime border with North Korea, killing 46 sailors. An international investigation later concluded that a North Korean torpedo was to blame, turning a dark night at sea into a flashpoint in modern geopolitics.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: A tragic sinking and tense aftermath is met with a reflective, hopeful episode about weathering storms and moving through dark periods.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 2017, anti-corruption protests erupted across Russia in at least 99 cities, many inspired by online investigations into elite wealth. Despite arrests and pressure, thousands of people—many of them young—filled squares to test how far they could push against entrenched power.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: A protest wave fueled by digital investigations and online mobilization pairs with an episode examining our relationship to technology and connectivity.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 1997, authorities found 39 bodies in a mansion near San Diego: members of the Heaven’s Gate group who had carried out a carefully choreographed mass suicide. They believed a spacecraft trailing the Hale–Bopp comet would take their souls to a higher plane, blending UFO lore with strict communal discipline.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

queue.match_reason: A group that constructed an extreme, alternate cosmic identity connects with an episode about alter-egos and building parallel realities—though in art, not in self-destruction.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 1021, the death of the enigmatic Fatimid caliph al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah was officially announced—six weeks after he vanished during a nighttime ride outside Cairo. His mysterious end, followed by the swift elevation of his son al-Zahir, helped fuel legends that he might one day return.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: A mysterious caliph’s disappearance in medieval Cairo fits with an evening journey that opens in an Arabic-sounding medina, full of secrecy and texture.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 1942, the first female prisoners arrived at Auschwitz concentration camp in German-occupied Poland. Many were political prisoners and resistance members, stepping into a system of industrialized cruelty that would claim millions of lives and redefine the meaning of atrocity.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

queue.match_reason: The arrival of women into one of history’s darkest places is honored with an episode celebrating powerful female voices and resilience.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 1979, as leaders signed peace in Washington, another quiet change unfolded at home: in many places, cassette tapes and cheap radios were making it easier than ever for ordinary people to record, trade, and replay the sounds of conflict and reconciliation. Though often overlooked, the humble tape helped turn peace accords, protest speeches, and new music into portable memories.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - secret_mp3_player_1
episode.description: Inspired by a throwback MP3 player on Instagram, Yasmin and Gaia take you on a nostalgic journey to the early 2000s. LimeWire, eMule, guilty-pleasure playlists — remember those? Hit play and relive the songs that shaped a generation.

queue.match_reason: A reflection on early portable recording and sharing of sound connects with an episode about later-era portable music culture and nostalgia for personal players.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 2010, as investigators later analyzed the Cheonan’s wreckage, they relied on tiny physical clues—chemical traces, fragment patterns, sonar echoes—to reconstruct an invisible underwater explosion. It’s a reminder that modern forensics is often less about eyewitnesses and more about reading the scars left in metal and water.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: Analyzing subtle traces under water pairs nicely with a cinematic, atmospheric episode that includes ‘Underwater Love’ and music built from small sampled fragments.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 1991—the same day Mercosur was born—musicians across Latin America were already blending borders sonically, long before trade deals did. From samba-reggae to rock en español, the region’s soundtracks have often anticipated the integration that politics struggles to catch up with.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: A fact about Latin America’s cross-border musical integration pairs with an episode devoted to Residente, a key voice in Latin alternative and political music.

queue.fact_text: On March 26, 2017, as Russians marched against corruption, activists used encrypted chats, memes, and viral videos to coordinate actions that once would have required underground print shops. It showcased how protest culture now lives as much in notification pings and livestreams as in city squares.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: A story about digital tools reshaping protest connects with an episode about a digital tool—Auto-Tune—that quietly transformed how voices are heard.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1958, the United States launched Explorer 3, a small satellite whose instruments helped confirm the existence of the Van Allen radiation belts around Earth. Built in just a few months, it carried a Geiger counter that quietly mapped invisible, high-energy particles trapped by our planet’s magnetic field.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal spaceflight milestone pairs with an episode themed around ‘space’ as possibility and creative shift.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1700, English navigator and naturalist William Dampier completed his circumnavigation of New Britain, proving it was an island separate from New Guinea. His logbooks combined pirate adventures with careful observations of plants, animals, and currents, making him one of the earliest scientist-explorers of the Pacific.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A maritime voyage of discovery fits with an episode literally recorded at sea and steeped in sailing imagery.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1812, a magnitude 7.7 earthquake struck near Caracas, Venezuela, devastating the city and surrounding valleys. Entire churches collapsed during Holy Thursday services, and the disaster reshaped both the landscape and the politics of a region already on the brink of independence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A sudden, disruptive ‘shock’ from the Earth is mirrored by high-voltage rock meant to jolt listeners awake.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1640, the Royal Academy of Turku was founded, becoming the first university in what is now Finland. For nearly two centuries it served as a northern outpost of scholarship, where Latin lectures echoed through wooden halls amid snow and Baltic winds.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: The founding of a university aligns directly with an episode devoted to the neuroscience and emotions of learning.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1939, Francoist forces launched the final offensive of the Spanish Civil War, a rapid campaign that crushed the last Republican strongholds. Within weeks, the conflict ended and Spain entered decades of dictatorship, leaving deep cultural and musical scars that later echoed through flamenco and protest songs.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: A pivotal Spanish political event is paired with an episode exploring Spain’s most expressive musical tradition.

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1954, the United States detonated the "Romeo" test at Bikini Atoll as part of Operation Castle, yielding about 11 megatons of explosive power. The blast was hundreds of times stronger than the bomb dropped on Hiroshima, painting the sky with a blinding fireball and casting new shadows over the ethics of nuclear testing.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: The imagery of a colossal nuclear "bomb" connects thematically with Sedna FM’s explosive first episode, titled "November Bomb."

published.fact_text: On March 26, 1885, the Métis people of the District of Saskatchewan, led by Louis Riel, began the North-West Rebellion against the Canadian government. It was a brief but intense uprising defending land, language, and identity on the prairies, and it still shapes debates about Indigenous rights in Canada today.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: An Indigenous-led rebellion for land and autonomy resonates with an episode about Tuareg music, exile, and resistance in another part of the world.
